hypothesis: If a 3D electron-density encoder is contrastively aligned to a pose-invariant SMILES encoder on paired molecules, then the learned 3D embeddings will become approximately invariant to rigid-body rotations and outperform unimodal and SE(3)-equivariant baselines on rotated retrieval and property prediction, because the contrastive objective forces different poses of the same molecule to map near a single SMILES representation.
